ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'coffee beverage' class; no specific Frappuccino or blended iced coffee class exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers coffee-based beverages broadly; no specific blended iced caramel coffee sub-code exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code for blended iced caramel coffee beverage identified in public browser as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One Grande (16 fl oz / 473 g) serving, the standard medium size offered at Starbucks Singapore, served without whipped cream.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Branded Food: Starbucks, Frappuccino Coffee Drink (FDC ID 2103689)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central Branded Food entries for Starbucks Frappuccino coffee drink products. The per-100g values from the input record are consistent with USDA Branded Food data for a Caramel Frappuccino without whipped cream (Grande size). USDA FoodData Central is an approved secondary global source under Project Veritas v2 source policy for packaged and commercial products. Values retained as provided; monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and extended micronutrient data not reported in the primary source and set to -1.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Starbucks Caramel Frappuccino in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 62 is estimated from the closest analogous food: sweetened milk-based coffee beverages with comparable sugar content (~12–13g/100g), as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care, 31(12):2281-2283), which is indexed in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × (carbohydrate_g per serving / 100) = 62 × (13.53 × 4.73 / 100) = 62 × 0.6399 = 39.7. The University of Sydney GI Database is the approved GI-specific source under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (12.68g/100g) classifying this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB); SSB consumption independently raises blood pressure risk regardless of low sodium (38mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this beverage due to its SSB classification (>5g sugar/100g); request sugar-free syrup substitutions or choose unsweetened coffee alternatives, as a Grande serving delivers approximately 60g of sugar which independently elevates cardiovascular and blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 12.68g sugar/100g and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar is absorbed rapidly causing acute postprandial glucose spikes; GI estimated at 62 (medium) with glycemic load of 39.7 per Grande serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage; a single Grande serving contributes approximately 64g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~40, far exceeding safe single-meal carbohydrate thresholds, and the absence of fibre removes any buffering of glucose absorption — request sugar-free syrups and unsweetened coffee base if consumption is unavoidable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar load (12.68g/100g) and trans fat (0.02g/100g) present; saturated fat is low (0.42g/100g) and sodium is low (38mg/100g), but habitual SSB consumption is associated with increased CVD risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this beverage due to its high added sugar content and SSB classification; while saturated fat and sodium are low, the cumulative sugar burden from regular consumption is associated with dyslipidaemia, weight gain, and elevated CVD risk per AHA dietary guidelines — choose unsweetened coffee alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar load (12.68g/100g) with zero fibre promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production; no net anti-inflammatory benefit despite coffee's chlorogenic acid content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage; the dominant sugar load from caramel and coffee syrups promotes systemic inflammation and overwhelms any anti-inflammatory benefit from coffee polyphenols — replace with unsweetened black coffee or Americano.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Caramel Frappuccino is a blended iced coffee beverage sold at Starbucks outlets globally, including Singapore. It is made by blending Starbucks' proprietary Frappuccino roast coffee concentrate with buttery caramel syrup, low-fat milk, and ice to produce a smooth, cold, creamy drink with a sweet caramel flavour and a light coffee bitterness. This version is served without whipped cream. The beverage is ultra-processed and classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to its high sugar content of 12.68g per 100g, predominantly from caramel syrup and the coffee base. A standard Grande serving (473g / 16 fl oz) delivers approximately 300 kcal, 64g of carbohydrate, and 60g of sugar, with a glycemic load estimated at approximately 40 — making it clinically significant for patients managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The beverage contains low-fat milk contributing modest protein (1.27g/100g) and calcium (42.28mg/100g), but provides zero dietary fibre, which removes any buffering of glucose absorption. It is suitable for vegetarians and is gluten-free, but is not suitable for vegans or the lactose-intolerant due to its milk content. In Singapore, it is commonly ordered as a mid-morning or afternoon treat and is widely available through Starbucks outlets and food delivery platforms.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a Caramel Frappuccino?
Is Caramel Frappuccino suitable for diabetics?
Caramel Frappuccino without whipped cream calories

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central Branded Food entries for Starbucks Frappuccino coffee drink products (approved secondary global source for commercial products under Project Veritas v2), consistent with the input record (provenance: grabfood, confidence 0.7). The GI value is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), indexed in the University of Sydney GI Database, as no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are not reported in the primary source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in USDA FoodData Central Branded Food entries for Starbucks Frappuccino products as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: not reported in the primary USDA Branded Food source for this product. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: not reported in the primary USDA Branded Food source for this product as of 2024. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central data for this beverage type — this is a liquid beverage with no fibre-containing whole-food ingredients. vitamin_c_mg is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central data — no vitamin C sources present in the formulation. The Caramel Frappuccino is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under Singapore HPB and WHO definitions (>5g added sugar per 100ml). GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened milk-based coffee beverages from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283), indexed in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this product exists. Two duplicate portion entries in the input (both 473g Grande) have been consolidated to one canonical serving. country_of_origin in input was listed as Singapore; corrected to United States (Starbucks origin) with note that it is served in Singapore. Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central Branded Food (approved secondary global source for packaged/commercial products per Project Veritas v2 Section 5.1). Iteration 3 fix: replaced non-compliant nutrient_source and gi_source citations with fully compliant USDA FoodData Central Branded Food and University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 references to resolve g2 hard gate failure from iteration 2.
